name: influenced_genre__dream_pop__luminous_otherworldly_pop
description: Outgoing work-to-genre defining-template claim. Subject: Heaven or Las Vegas (1990). Object concept: dream pop. Aspect: luminous otherworldly pop, lush production, emotionally legible but linguistically elusive vocals and effortless dreaminess. Direction: tagged work → later dream-pop practice. The cited sources describe Cocteau Twins as defining dream pop; this is a canonical-template claim rather than sole authorship.

name: influenced_genre__dream_pop__reverb_voice_as_emotional_texture
description: Outgoing work-to-genre precursor claim. Subject: Head Over Heels (1983). Object concept: dream pop. Aspect: cavernous reverb, guitar as atmosphere, voice as nonliteral emotional texture and escape into a self-contained dream world. Direction: tagged work → later dream-pop practice. The cited review describes the album as creating the band’s enduring dream-world aesthetic; it does not claim sole invention.

name: influenced_genre__electro_industrial__mechanical_rhythm_tape_collage
description: Outgoing work-to-genre consolidation claim. Subject: Red Mecca (1981). Object concept: electro-industrial. Aspect: hypnotic mechanical rhythm, disorienting tape collage, processed voice and bleak electronic atmosphere. Direction: tagged work → later electro-industrial practice. The cited review credits Cabaret Voltaire with pioneering the form and presents Red Mecca as a dark mature landmark, not its sole origin.

name: influenced_genre__extreme_metal__hyperspeed_punk_metal_template
description: Outgoing work-to-genre influence claim. Subject: Ace of Spades (1980). Object concept: extreme metal. Aspect: hyperspeed punkish metal, chaotic bass/guitar unison, wild drumming and a heavy but catchy attack. Direction: tagged work → later faster extreme-metal practice. The source presents this as a major evolutionary example, not a sole-origin claim.

name: influenced_genre__extreme_metal__speed_distortion_bridge
description: Outgoing work-to-genre influence claim. Subject: Overkill (1979). Object concept: extreme metal. Aspect: speed-metal aggression, distorted lead-bass attack, double-kick propulsion and punk appetite for direct rock and roll. Direction: tagged work → later extreme-metal practice. Metal Archives community reviews call the album an incalculable influence while also stressing continuity with earlier Motörhead.

name: influenced_genre__glam_rock__erotic_stomp_template

name: influenced_genre__gothic_rock__drum_machine_bass_vampiric_template
description: Outgoing work-to-genre defining-template claim. Subject: First and Last and Always (1985). Object concept: gothic rock. Aspect: stark drum machine, warped psychedelic guitar, menacing melodic bass and alienated vampiric vocal delivery. Direction: tagged work → later gothic-rock practice. The cited review says this identity formed its own strand in goth’s DNA; it does not claim the album created the entire genre.

name: influenced_genre__gothic_rock__serrated_theatrical_template
description: Outgoing work-to-genre foundational-template claim. Subject: In the Flat Field (1980). Object concept: gothic rock. Aspect: serrated post-punk guitar, theatrical vocal extremity, expressionist horror and art-school violence. Direction: tagged work → later gothic-rock practice. The sources describe Bauhaus as genre-defining while retaining their broader post-punk identity; this is not a sole-origin claim.

name: influenced_genre__gothic_rock__synth_choir_maximalism
description: Outgoing work-to-genre expansion claim. Subject: Floodland (1987). Object concept: gothic rock. Aspect: murky synth foundation, huge programmed drums, choir, feedback-linked suite form and Jim Steinman-scale pop maximalism. Direction: tagged work → later monumental gothic-rock production. The source presents the album as a defining masterpiece and expansion beyond the debut’s guitar-centered template.

name: influenced_genre__grunge__primordial_caveman_blues

Aspect: extremely reduced 'caveman blues', repetitive distorted riffs and confrontational physical simplicity. Direction: tagged work → later grunge practice. The cited review groups the early Stooges records as touchstones rather than claiming this album alone created grunge.

name: influenced_genre__hard_rock__raw_aggression
description: Outgoing work-to-genre influence claim. Subject: Raw Power (1973). Object concept: later hard rock. Aspect: raw aggression, charismatic immediacy and guitar-forward abrasion. Direction: tagged work → later hard-rock practice, especially the 1980s line identified by the source.

name: influenced_genre__hip_hop__rhythmic_vocal_delivery
description: Outgoing work-to-genre precursor claim. Subject: Suicide (1977). Object concept: hip-hop vocal practice. Aspect: Alan Vega’s rhythmic, cadence-led delivery over repetitive machine accompaniment. Direction: tagged work → later hip-hop practice. The cited critic calls this embryonic rather than claiming direct, universal or sole causation.

name: influenced_genre__industrial_music__machine_pulse_confrontation
description: Outgoing work-to-genre precursor claim. Subject: Suicide (1977). Object concept: industrial music. Aspect: machine pulse, repetition, electronic abrasion and physically confrontational performance. Direction: tagged work → later industrial practice. name: influenced_genre__industrial_music__percussive_production
description: Outgoing work-to-genre influence or precedent. Object concept: industrial music. Aspect: percussion-led construction, distortion and hostile studio texture. Direction: tagged work → later industrial practice. Evidence context: the cited source calls the tagged album a pivotal forerunner; it does not classify the album itself as industrial music.

name: influenced_genre__industrial_music__scrap_metal_tool_percussion
description: Outgoing work-to-genre foundational-template claim. Subject: Kollaps (1981). Object concept: industrial music. Aspect: scrap-metal and power-tool percussion, feral rhythmic noise and a factory-mishap sound world. Direction: tagged work → later industrial practice. The sources emphasize Neubauten’s leadership and many imitators; the tag does not claim industrial music began with this album.

name: influenced_genre__japanese_cyberpunk__diy_mechanized_punk_city
description: Outgoing work-to-genre precursor claim. Subject: Burst City (1982). Object concept: Japanese cyberpunk cinema. Aspect: dystopian mechanized city, DIY steel equipment, modified bodies and clothing, undercranked speed montage, punk performance and industrial revolt. Direction: tagged work → later Japanese cyberpunk practice. The cited essay explicitly identifies the film as setting the plane for the movement and influencing Akira and Tetsuo; it does not claim Burst City as the only origin.

name: influenced_genre__math_rock__precision_and_angular_rhythm
description: Outgoing work-to-genre precursor claim. Subject: This Heat (1979). Object concept: math rock. Aspect: exact ensemble control, angular rhythmic organization and abrupt formal changes emerging from experimental-rock practice. Direction: tagged work → later math-rock practice. The source identifies seeds rather than sole invention.

name: influenced_genre__meta_horror__fiction_reality_collapse
description: Outgoing work-to-genre precedent claim. Subject is the tagged work, Wes Craven’s New Nightmare (1994). Object concept: later meta-horror. Aspect: creators and performers playing versions of themselves while a fictional horror entity crosses into production reality. Direction: tagged work → later meta-horror practice. The sources present the film as groundwork for Craven’s subsequent self-aware horror; it is not claimed to be the first self-reflexive horror film.

name: influenced_genre__meta_slasher__self_aware_rules_and_genuine_scares

Aspect: characters verbalizing genre rules while the film still produces sincere suspense, violence and emotional stakes. Direction: tagged work → later meta-slasher practice. The source describes the film as genre-defining and widely imitated; it does not claim invention of all self-aware horror.

name: influenced_genre__modern_zombie_film__flesh_eating_ghoul_template
description: Outgoing work-to-genre founding-template claim. Subject is the tagged work, Night of the Living Dead (1968). Object concept: the modern zombie film. Aspect: anonymous reanimated flesh-eating ghouls, epidemic social collapse, siege survival and human conflict under crisis. Direction: tagged work → later modern zombie cinema. The sources credit Romero with establishing the modern flesh-eating-ghoul model; the tag does not claim that earlier zombie films did not exist.

name: influenced_genre__new_wave__minimal_keyboard_attitude

The cited review locates roots of new wave in Suicide’s first two albums; this tag does not claim sole invention or influence on every new-wave style.

name: influenced_genre__noise_rock__feral_freeform_attack
description: Outgoing work-to-genre precursor claim. Subject: Fun House (1970). Object concept: noise rock. Aspect: feral live-room performance, distortion, escalating free-form saxophone and breakdown of stable rock arrangement. Direction: tagged work → later noise-rock practice. The cited source treats the album as a major touchstone, not the sole origin of noise rock.

name: influenced_genre__noise_rock__inhuman_rhythm_sheet_metal_guitar
description: Outgoing work-to-genre influence claim. Subject: Filth (1983). Object concept: noise rock. Aspect: inhuman repetitive rhythm, sheet-metal-like guitar attack, no-wave dissonance and bodily volume. Direction: tagged work → later noise-rock practice. The source identifies later bands taking up these traits; it does not claim Filth as the only origin.
